title: Quantitative easing
summary: The Bank of England provides an educational overview of quantitative easing (QE), a monetary policy tool used to lower long-term borrowing costs when Bank Rate is already very low. The UK first implemented QE in March 2009 during the Global Financial Crisis, ultimately purchasing £895 billion in bonds (£875bn government gilts, £20bn corporate bonds). QT (quantitative tightening) began in February 2022 to reduce the bond stock, with the stated aim of preserving the ability to use QE again in future rather than directly targeting interest rates or inflation.
key_points: QE is the Bank of England's secondary monetary policy tool after Bank Rate, used to lower long-term interest rates when Bank Rate cannot be lowered further
The Bank purchased £875 billion of UK government bonds (gilts) and £20 billion of UK corporate bonds, totaling £895 billion
QE was first used in March 2009 during the Global Financial Crisis when Bank Rate could not be lowered further
The last QE expansion was announced in November 2020, and QT began in February 2022
QE works by increasing bond prices, which lowers bond yields that serve as benchmark rates for mortgages and other financial products
Research cited by the Bank suggests QE was most effective during stressed market conditions: post-2009, post-2016 EU referendum, and spring 2020 pandemic
The Bank states its research finds the 'overwhelming majority' of people benefited from QE when considering both wealth and employment effects
why_it_matters: This page provides the Bank of England's official framework for understanding QE and QT. While it contains no new policy announcements, it establishes the institutional narrative that QE is intended to support inflation toward the 2% target, is distinct from active interest rate policy, and that QT's purpose is preserving future QE capacity rather than being inflationary or deflationary in itself. This framing is relevant for interpreting future BoE policy communications.
macro_context: QE lowers long-term borrowing costs by pushing down bond yields, which stimulates spending and puts upward pressure on inflation. QT reverses this by allowing bonds to mature and selling others, removing money from the economy. The BoE characterizes these as distinct operations: QE supports inflation when it is too low; QT aims to restore policy flexibility, not to tighten conditions directly.
market_structure_context: The page explains that the BoE creates central bank reserves digitally to purchase bonds, and that QT causes these reserves and associated money to disappear when bonds mature or are sold. This describes the basic mechanics of the BoE's balance sheet operations.
